title: Problem (?) with HybridNew --LHCmode LHC-feldman-cousins and 3 independent signal strengths

posts.name: Alexandre Nikitenko
posts.text:
Dear experts,
thanks to Aliya and Andrew Gilbert I got instructions how to evaluate p-value with toys in case I have 3 independent signal strengths.
I run
combine 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root -M HybridNew --LHCmode LHC-feldman-cousins --clsAcc 0 --singlePoint r_sig1=0,r_sig2=0,r_sig3=0 --saveToys --fullBToys --saveHybridResult -T 10000 -i 1 -s -1 --setParameters mean=27.3,width=1.4 --freezeParameters mean,width --setParameterRanges r_sig1=0.0,120.0:r_sig2=0.0,120.0,r_sig3=0.0,120.0
and got p-value 0.01126 which corresponds to significance of 2.30
This p-value is 10 times larger than I got using analytical formula eq (4) from
[2109.13426] Comment on “Accumulating Evidence for the Associate Production of a Neutral Scalar with Mass around 151 GeV” (arxiv.org)
pvalue = 0.125*(3.0TMath::Prob(2deltaNLL,1)+3.0TMath::Prob(2deltaNLL,2)+1.0TMath::Prob(2deltaNLL,3));
for my deltaNLL = 6.53 corresponding p-value = 0.00121802 and significance is 3.0.
deltaNLL I got running:
combine -M MultiDimFit --algo fixed --fixedPointPOIs r_sig1=0,r_sig2=0,r_sig3=0 --redefineSignalPOIs r_sig1,r_sig2,r_sig3 --robustFit=1 --setParameters mean=27.3,width=1.4 --freezeParameters mean,width 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root -v 3
and extracting deltaNLL from output root file
higgsCombineTest.MultiDimFit.mH120.root
I do not beleive that factor of 10 difference in p-valus can be explained by the difference between analytical formula and toys. This formula was checked using toys by Andre David and Nick Wardle a while ago for 2 signal strengths.  Also individual significances are 3.1, 1.4 and 1.9 from individual fits of each SR.  I think combined significance of 3 is reasonable value.
I  think either I used wrongly combine feldman-cousins  or there is a bug in
feldman-cousins for few independent signal strenghts.
Please, help.
Thanks, Sasha.
posts.cooked_html:
Dear experts,

thanks to Aliya and Andrew Gilbert I got instructions how to evaluate p-value with toys in case I have 3 independent signal strengths.

I run

combine 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root -M HybridNew --LHCmode LHC-feldman-cousins --clsAcc 0 --singlePoint r_sig1=0,r_sig2=0,r_sig3=0 --saveToys --fullBToys --saveHybridResult -T 10000 -i 1 -s -1 --setParameters mean=27.3,width=1.4 --freezeParameters mean,width --setParameterRanges r_sig1=0.0,120.0:r_sig2=0.0,120.0,r_sig3=0.0,120.0

and got p-value 0.01126 which corresponds to significance of 2.30

This p-value is 10 times larger than I got using analytical formula eq (4) from

[2109.13426] Comment on “Accumulating Evidence for the Associate Production of a Neutral Scalar with Mass around 151 GeV” (arxiv.org)

pvalue = 0.125*(3.0TMath::Prob(2deltaNLL,1)+3.0TMath::Prob(2deltaNLL,2)+1.0TMath::Prob(2deltaNLL,3));

for my deltaNLL = 6.53 corresponding p-value = 0.00121802 and significance is 3.0.

deltaNLL I got running:

combine -M MultiDimFit --algo fixed --fixedPointPOIs r_sig1=0,r_sig2=0,r_sig3=0 --redefineSignalPOIs r_sig1,r_sig2,r_sig3 --robustFit=1 --setParameters mean=27.3,width=1.4 --freezeParameters mean,width 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root -v 3

and extracting deltaNLL from output root file

higgsCombineTest.MultiDimFit.mH120.root

I do not beleive that factor of 10 difference in p-valus can be explained by the difference between analytical formula and toys. This formula was checked using toys by Andre David and Nick Wardle a while ago for 2 signal strengths. Also individual significances are 3.1, 1.4 and 1.9 from individual fits of each SR. I think combined significance of 3 is reasonable value.

I think either I used wrongly combine feldman-cousins or there is a bug in
feldman-cousins for few independent signal strenghts.

Please, help.

Thanks, Sasha.

posts.name: Andrew Gilbert
posts.text:
Hi Sasha,
From an initial look, your command seems correct. Let me investigate a little whether this should work in principle - will get back to you in a couple of days.
Just to check, did you already verify the cases of the single signal strengths in the separate categories?
cheers,
Andrew
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Sasha,

From an initial look, your command seems correct. Let me investigate a little whether this should work in principle - will get back to you in a couple of days.

Just to check, did you already verify the cases of the single signal strengths in the separate categories?

cheers,
Andrew

posts.name: Alexandre Nikitenko
posts.text:
Hi Andrew,
yes, I did check that that feldman-cousins reproduces p-values I got with toys using usual command :
combine -M HybridNew  2017UL_dimuon_SR3.txt --LHCmode LHC-significance --saveToys --fullBToys --saveHybridResult -T 5000 -i 1 -s -1 --setParameters mean=27.2,width=4.9 --freezeParameters mean,width
Thanks, Sasha.
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Andrew,

yes, I did check that that feldman-cousins reproduces p-values I got with toys using usual command :

combine -M HybridNew 2017UL_dimuon_SR3.txt --LHCmode LHC-significance --saveToys --fullBToys --saveHybridResult -T 5000 -i 1 -s -1 --setParameters mean=27.2,width=4.9 --freezeParameters mean,width

Thanks, Sasha.

posts.name: Nicholas Wardle
posts.text:
Hi Sasha,
I thought it would be good to try this on a simple set of datacards checking to see if our Feldman-Cousins method (i.e the one you used) gives the same result as the Fowlie paper in the case of 1, 2 or 3 parameters.
Attached is a pdf showing the results I got and the datacards/commands I used. In short, everthing checks out so there is no bug in the method we recommended. Something else must be going on, or for this particular case, the Fowlie formulae do not hold
AsymptoticSignificance.pdf (179.2 KB)
Can you take a look and see if you can spot differences (of course in my case I don;t have a mass and width parameter to freeze, but that should not be an issue)?
Best,
Nick
[1] this could of course be just that we are not in the asymptotic limit or that there are correlations between the signal strengths which the  Fowlie paper assumes are zero. I do agree that a factor of 10 is hard to understand from these however.
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Sasha,

I thought it would be good to try this on a simple set of datacards checking to see if our Feldman-Cousins method (i.e the one you used) gives the same result as the Fowlie paper in the case of 1, 2 or 3 parameters.

Attached is a pdf showing the results I got and the datacards/commands I used. In short, everthing checks out so there is no bug in the method we recommended. Something else must be going on, or for this particular case, the Fowlie formulae do not hold

AsymptoticSignificance.pdf (179.2 KB)

Can you take a look and see if you can spot differences (of course in my case I don;t have a mass and width parameter to freeze, but that should not be an issue)?

Best,
Nick

[1] this could of course be just that we are not in the asymptotic limit or that there are correlations between the signal strengths which the Fowlie paper assumes are zero. I do agree that a factor of 10 is hard to understand from these however.

posts.name: Nicholas Wardle
posts.text:
Just a small edit - the first figure should of course read \frac{1}{2}\delta(0) +\frac{1}{2}\chi^{2}(1) in the legend (and that is what is actually plotted in the red line)
Best,
Nick

posts.name: Alexandre Nikitenko
posts.text:
Hi Nick,
Thanks for checking !
I do not use
–redefineSignalPOI r,r2,r3 -i 10 -n r3
what does
-n r3
mean ?
May be this difference is a reason …
Cheers, Sasha.
posts.cooked_html:

-n r3

posts.name: Nicholas Wardle
posts.text:
Hi Sasha,
Ah,
The option –redefineSignalPOI r,r2,r3 tells combine to set the list of parameters of interest to r,r2,r3 when defining the test statistic. I am not sure if your datacards/model is already doing that but you could try adding this option for your 3 parameters that are your signal strengths.
The option -n r3 is just naming the output file so doesn’t affect the results at all,
Best,
Nick
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Sasha,

Ah,

The option –redefineSignalPOI r,r2,r3 tells combine to set the list of parameters of interest to r,r2,r3 when defining the test statistic. I am not sure if your datacards/model is already doing that but you could try adding this option for your 3 parameters that are your signal strengths.

The option -n r3 is just naming the output file so doesn’t affect the results at all,

Best,

Nick

posts.name: Alexandre Nikitenko
posts.text:
great !
Can you send me command how do you read merged output files to get final p-value from many runs. What is written on page did not work for me. I have send
question to Aliya through CMS Talk
Combination of three Search Regions using toys and three independent signal strengths - Common Analysis Tools / Statistical Interpretation Tools - CMS talk (cern.ch)
it is at the bottom.
Thanks !
Sasha.

Combination of three Search Regions using toys and three independent signal strengths - Common Analysis Tools / Statistical Interpretation Tools - CMS talk (cern.ch)

it is at the bottom.

posts.name: Nicholas Wardle
posts.text:
Hi Sasha,
You just need to include the same option --redefineSignalPOIs and replace --grid=input.root with --toysFile input.root --singlePoint (since you only have a single point, not a grid of points)
Best,
nick
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Sasha,

You just need to include the same option --redefineSignalPOIs and replace --grid=input.root with --toysFile input.root --singlePoint (since you only have a single point, not a grid of points)
Best,
nick

posts.name: Alexandre Nikitenko
posts.text:
Hi Nick,
unfortunately adding
–redefineSignalPOI r_sig1,r_sig2,r_sig3
did not help for me, I got again p-value 0.011 instead of 0.0012 I got from Fowlie paper.  Signal strengths are not correlated at all.  I think I do something wrong…
If you have a bit of free time, can you check what I do ?  I have combined cards
for 2018, SR1, SR2, SR3 at
/afs/cern.ch/work/a/anikiten/public/CMSSW_11_3_4/src/HiggsAnalysis/CombinedLimit/test/2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.txt
and at the same dir I have 2018UL_RooFitWorkspaceSR1.root, 2018UL_RooFitWorkspaceSR2.root, 2018UL_RooFitWorkspaceSR3.root
I then create a physics model with

text2workspace.py -P HiggsAnalysis.CombinedLimit.PhysicsModel:multiSignalModel --PO verbose --PO ‘map=SR1_2018/sig:r_sig1[1,0,120]’ --PO ‘map=SR2_2018/sig:r_sig2[1,0,120]’ --PO ‘map=SR3_2018/sig:r_sig3[1,0,120]’ 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.txt -o 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root

and finally run Feldmab-Cousins:

combine 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root -M HybridNew --LHCmode LHC-feldman-cousins --clsAcc 0 --singlePoint r_sig1=0,r_sig2=0,r_sig3=0 --saveToys --fullBToys --saveHybridResult -T 5000 -i 1 -s -1 --setParameters mean=27.3,width=1.4 --freezeParameters mean,width --setParameterRanges r_sig1=0.0,120.0:r_sig2=0.0,120.0:r_sig3=0.0,120.0 --redefineSignalPOI r_sig1,r_sig2,r_sig3

In the mean time I will try to run it with cards done in your way, without step 1.
Thank you very much for all your help !
Cheers, Sasha.
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Nick,

unfortunately adding

–redefineSignalPOI r_sig1,r_sig2,r_sig3

did not help for me, I got again p-value 0.011 instead of 0.0012 I got from Fowlie paper. Signal strengths are not correlated at all. I think I do something wrong…

If you have a bit of free time, can you check what I do ? I have combined cards
for 2018, SR1, SR2, SR3 at

/afs/cern.ch/work/a/anikiten/public/CMSSW_11_3_4/src/HiggsAnalysis/CombinedLimit/test/2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.txt

and at the same dir I have 2018UL_RooFitWorkspaceSR1.root, 2018UL_RooFitWorkspaceSR2.root, 2018UL_RooFitWorkspaceSR3.root

I then create a physics model with

  1. text2workspace.py -P HiggsAnalysis.CombinedLimit.PhysicsModel:multiSignalModel --PO verbose --PO ‘map=SR1_2018/sig:r_sig1[1,0,120]’ --PO ‘map=SR2_2018/sig:r_sig2[1,0,120]’ --PO ‘map=SR3_2018/sig:r_sig3[1,0,120]’ 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.txt -o 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root

and finally run Feldmab-Cousins:

  1. combine 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root -M HybridNew --LHCmode LHC-feldman-cousins --clsAcc 0 --singlePoint r_sig1=0,r_sig2=0,r_sig3=0 --saveToys --fullBToys --saveHybridResult -T 5000 -i 1 -s -1 --setParameters mean=27.3,width=1.4 --freezeParameters mean,width --setParameterRanges r_sig1=0.0,120.0:r_sig2=0.0,120.0:r_sig3=0.0,120.0 --redefineSignalPOI r_sig1,r_sig2,r_sig3

In the mean time I will try to run it with cards done in your way, without step 1.

Thank you very much for all your help !

Cheers, Sasha.

posts.name: Nicholas Wardle
posts.text:
Hi Sasha,
Since you pointed me to your datacard, i’ve had a go myself and here is what I did

I copied over your files to my own local area
I run the same text2workspace command (I see now why adding the --redefineSignalPOI didn’t change things since you already used a physics model to define them, I didn’t realise that before, but in any case I left the command in since it doesn’t hurt) :
text2workspace.py -P HiggsAnalysis.CombinedLimit.PhysicsModel:multiSignalModel --PO verbose --PO 'map=SR1_2018/sig:r_sig1[1,0,120]' --PO 'map=SR2_2018/sig:r_sig2[1,0,120]' --PO 'map=SR3_2018/sig:r_sig3[1,0,120]' 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.txt -o 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root
I created a .sh file with the combine line needed to generate the toys which looks like below,

#!/bin/bash
cd /afs/cern.ch/work/n/nckw/combine-versions/113x/CMSSW_11_3_4/src/HiggsAnalysis/CombinedLimit/forSasha/comb/jobs/
cmsenv

i=$1

combine ../2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root -M HybridNew --LHCmode LHC-feldman-cousins --clsAcc 0 --singlePoint r_sig1=0,r_sig2=0,r_sig3=0 --saveToys --saveHybridResult -T 500 -s -1 --setParameters mean=27.3,width=1.4 --freezeParameters mean,width,sigma --setParameterRanges r_sig1=0.0,120.0:r_sig2=0.0,120.0:r_sig3=0.0,120.0 --redefineSignalPOI r_sig1,r_sig2,r_sig3 -n run_${i} --cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy 0

Note that it also fixes the parameter sigma since the range was set to not allow it to float anyway (easier just to make it constant). I also used --cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy 0 since it makes the fits for the toys a bit quicker.
I then submitted about 50 of these jobs to get 25000 toys in total (takes about the time it took to make a coffee and read a few emails etc).
Then I collected the output from the jobs together with hadd into a single file which I called toys.root and I run the following command
combine 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root -M HybridNew --LHCmode LHC-feldman-cousins --clsAcc 0 --singlePoint r_sig1=0,r_sig2=0,r_sig3=0 --readHybridResult -s -1 --setParameters mean=27.3,width=1.4 --freezeParameters mean,width,sigma --setParameterRanges r_sig1=0.0,120.0:r_sig2=0.0,120.0:r_sig3=0.0,120.0 --redefineSignalPOI r_sig1,r_sig2,r_sig3 -n result --cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy 0 --toysFile toys.root

and the output is

-- Hybrid New --
Pmu = 0.002 +/- 0.00028256
Done in 0.00 min (cpu), 0.01 min (real)

Finally, since I had the script available to do it, I also plotted the distribution of the toys and the formulae from Fowlie on top (see below).

As you can see, things look nice - in my calculation, I got 2DeltaNLL=12.72. Plugging into the Fowlie formulae for n=3, I get a p-value of 0.00144 so pretty close to what we get from the toys. I am running a few more toys now just to see if the agreement gets better, but to me given the agreement between the line and the histogram I would say we’re ok.
So, with the  toys calculation (p-value=0.002), one would say the significance is  2.88 sigma.
Best,
Nick
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Sasha,

Since you pointed me to your datacard, i’ve had a go myself and here is what I did

  1. I copied over your files to my own local area
  2. I run the same text2workspace command (I see now why adding the --redefineSignalPOI didn’t change things since you already used a physics model to define them, I didn’t realise that before, but in any case I left the command in since it doesn’t hurt) :
    text2workspace.py -P HiggsAnalysis.CombinedLimit.PhysicsModel:multiSignalModel --PO verbose --PO 'map=SR1_2018/sig:r_sig1[1,0,120]' --PO 'map=SR2_2018/sig:r_sig2[1,0,120]' --PO 'map=SR3_2018/sig:r_sig3[1,0,120]' 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.txt -o 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root
  3. I created a .sh file with the combine line needed to generate the toys which looks like below,
#!/bin/bash
cd /afs/cern.ch/work/n/nckw/combine-versions/113x/CMSSW_11_3_4/src/HiggsAnalysis/CombinedLimit/forSasha/comb/jobs/
cmsenv

i=$1

combine ../2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root -M HybridNew --LHCmode LHC-feldman-cousins --clsAcc 0 --singlePoint r_sig1=0,r_sig2=0,r_sig3=0 --saveToys --saveHybridResult -T 500 -s -1 --setParameters mean=27.3,width=1.4 --freezeParameters mean,width,sigma --setParameterRanges r_sig1=0.0,120.0:r_sig2=0.0,120.0:r_sig3=0.0,120.0 --redefineSignalPOI r_sig1,r_sig2,r_sig3 -n run_${i} --cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy 0

Note that it also fixes the parameter sigma since the range was set to not allow it to float anyway (easier just to make it constant). I also used --cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy 0 since it makes the fits for the toys a bit quicker.

I then submitted about 50 of these jobs to get 25000 toys in total (takes about the time it took to make a coffee and read a few emails etc).

Then I collected the output from the jobs together with hadd into a single file which I called toys.root and I run the following command

combine 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root -M HybridNew --LHCmode LHC-feldman-cousins --clsAcc 0 --singlePoint r_sig1=0,r_sig2=0,r_sig3=0 --readHybridResult -s -1 --setParameters mean=27.3,width=1.4 --freezeParameters mean,width,sigma --setParameterRanges r_sig1=0.0,120.0:r_sig2=0.0,120.0:r_sig3=0.0,120.0 --redefineSignalPOI r_sig1,r_sig2,r_sig3 -n result --cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy 0 --toysFile toys.root

and the output is


 -- Hybrid New --
Pmu = 0.002 +/- 0.00028256
Done in 0.00 min (cpu), 0.01 min (real)

Finally, since I had the script available to do it, I also plotted the distribution of the toys and the formulae from Fowlie on top (see below).

As you can see, things look nice - in my calculation, I got 2DeltaNLL=12.72. Plugging into the Fowlie formulae for n=3, I get a p-value of 0.00144 so pretty close to what we get from the toys. I am running a few more toys now just to see if the agreement gets better, but to me given the agreement between the line and the histogram I would say we’re ok.

So, with the toys calculation (p-value=0.002), one would say the significance is 2.88 sigma.

Best,
Nick

posts.name: Alexandre Nikitenko
posts.text:
Hi Nick,
thanks !  Significance for your p-value=0.002190 is 2.85.
As you said before your 2DeltaNLL=12.72.
However when I run command:
combine -M MultiDimFit --algo fixed --fixedPointPOIs r_sig1=0,r_sig2=0,r_sig3=0 --redefineSignalPOIs r_sig1,r_sig2,r_sig3 --robustFit=1 --setParameters mean=27.3,width=1.4 --freezeParameters mean,width,sigma 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root -v 3
I read from outgoing root file  deltaNLL=6.53488.
So, 2 x  6.53488 =  13.07,  larger than yours value.  With Fowlie formulae it gives
significance 3.0.
How we can understand different deltaNLL obtained in feldman-cousins and
with MultiDimFit ?  Sure, I prefer to have significance 3.0
Cheers, Sasha.
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Nick,

thanks ! Significance for your p-value=0.002190 is 2.85.

As you said before your 2DeltaNLL=12.72.

However when I run command:

combine -M MultiDimFit --algo fixed --fixedPointPOIs r_sig1=0,r_sig2=0,r_sig3=0 --redefineSignalPOIs r_sig1,r_sig2,r_sig3 --robustFit=1 --setParameters mean=27.3,width=1.4 --freezeParameters mean,width,sigma 2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root -v 3

I read from outgoing root file deltaNLL=6.53488.
So, 2 x 6.53488 = 13.07, larger than yours value. With Fowlie formulae it gives
significance 3.0.

How we can understand different deltaNLL obtained in feldman-cousins and
with MultiDimFit ? Sure, I prefer to have significance 3.0

Cheers, Sasha.

posts.name: Nicholas Wardle
posts.text:
Hi again,
The difference in the value of 2*deltaNLL comes from the fact I am using the --cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy 0 option which, as I mentioned, makes the fit quicker but is less accurate
If I use the same options as you, I get your value of 2*deltaNLL. So I don’t think there’s an issue, the options you’ve used should give a more accurate answer
Also in this game I think 2.85 sigma is not materially less interesting than 3!
Best
Nick

posts.text:

“The y axis is not the p-value, it is the density, the p-value is the integral above the black line”
Best
Nick

posts.name: Alexandre Nikitenko
posts.text:
Hi Nick,
but I use this formulae from Fowlie
pvalue = 0.125*(3.0TMath::Prob(2 deltaNLL,1)+3.0TMath::Prob(2 deltaNLL,2)+1.0TMath::Prob(2 deltaNLL,3));
for p-value calculation… I just realized that it is indeed probability density function, not integral above observed chi^2 (of 2 x deltaNLL).  Shell I use then
integral from this function to compare with p-value given by toys ?
Thanks, Sasha.
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Nick,

but I use this formulae from Fowlie

pvalue = 0.125*(3.0TMath::Prob(2 deltaNLL,1)+3.0TMath::Prob(2 deltaNLL,2)+1.0TMath::Prob(2 deltaNLL,3));

for p-value calculation… I just realized that it is indeed probability density function, not integral above observed chi^2 (of 2 x deltaNLL). Shell I use then
integral from this function to compare with p-value given by toys ?

Thanks, Sasha.

posts.name: Nicholas Wardle
posts.text:
Hi Sasha,
The formulae you used is correct since TMath::Prob(x,n) gives the integral from x to infinity of a chi-square distribution with n degrees of freedom already,
Best,
Nick
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Sasha,

The formulae you used is correct since TMath::Prob(x,n) gives the integral from x to infinity of a chi-square distribution with n degrees of freedom already,

Best,
Nick

posts.name: Alexandre Nikitenko
posts.text:
Hi Nick,
it seems we do not have yet “happy end” of this story.
I reproduced your p-value about of 0.002 with 25K toys using option you used:
–cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy 0
Olga then run feldman-cousins without this option and got p-value about of 0.01
with 1 M toys…
I run it myself again with 25K toys without this option and reproduced
her p-value about of 0.01.
So, we see different in p-value by factor of 5 when we run feldman-cousins with
and without option --cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy 0
Can you understand it ?
Cheers, Sasha.

posts.name: Nicholas Wardle
posts.text:
Hi Sasha,
Hmm, seems there’s more to think about. I took a look at removing the option and compared the distributions (see the figure below). The black is with the option --cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy=0 and the blue is without it. In both cases, there is a rather large tail in the distribution (now I am plotting much further in the test statistic distribution) but the tail is much larger for the blue. I am running a few jobs now to see if I can spot what’s causing this.
One thing I noticed is that in many cases, I see warnings from combine like this
p.d.f value is less than zero (-109.565970), forcing value to zero @ x=dimuonmass12=49.6061, coefList=(a1_18sr2 = 7.839,a2_18sr2 = 0.640501,a3_18sr2 = -0.0342118,a4_18sr2 = 0.000346907)

and in some cases, that value is very large. Now usually the fitter will try to walk away from this region but i’m not certain that it’s always managing to do it which could lead to a lot of fit failures. I’m not immediately sure why the option makes a difference but others might have some idea about it.
One way we avoided this in H->gg is we used Bernstein Polynomials defined such that the background can never be negative. Did you ever consider these before instead of standard RooPolynomial or RooChebychev ?
I’ll let you know if I get any further checking this odd tail,
Nick
Screenshot 2024-04-29 at 09.35.101202×872 70.3 KB
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Sasha,

Hmm, seems there’s more to think about. I took a look at removing the option and compared the distributions (see the figure below). The black is with the option --cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy=0 and the blue is without it. In both cases, there is a rather large tail in the distribution (now I am plotting much further in the test statistic distribution) but the tail is much larger for the blue. I am running a few jobs now to see if I can spot what’s causing this.

One thing I noticed is that in many cases, I see warnings from combine like this

 p.d.f value is less than zero (-109.565970), forcing value to zero @ x=dimuonmass12=49.6061, coefList=(a1_18sr2 = 7.839,a2_18sr2 = 0.640501,a3_18sr2 = -0.0342118,a4_18sr2 = 0.000346907)

and in some cases, that value is very large. Now usually the fitter will try to walk away from this region but i’m not certain that it’s always managing to do it which could lead to a lot of fit failures. I’m not immediately sure why the option makes a difference but others might have some idea about it.

One way we avoided this in H->gg is we used Bernstein Polynomials defined such that the background can never be negative. Did you ever consider these before instead of standard RooPolynomial or RooChebychev ?

I’ll let you know if I get any further checking this odd tail,
Nick

Screenshot 2024-04-29 at 09.35.101202×872 70.3 KB

We changed Bernstein to Chevychev for SR3 on request of Stat.Com. since Bernstein gave a very large correlation for bkg. parameters.
Cheers, Sasha.

posts.name: Nicholas Wardle
posts.text:
Hi Sasha,
I think if we stick with –cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy 0 , (which makes sense given that the things being run without it shouldn’t even be necessary for this), then we really have to also use the same when calculating the 2xdeltaNLL. We can’t just pick numbers that give us the largest significance and claim that we really understand that result,
Nick
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Sasha,

We can’t just pick numbers that give us the largest significance and claim that we really understand that result,

Nick

posts.name: Nicholas Wardle
posts.text:
Hi Sasha,
I was trying out a few more tests with toys, and I am noticing that the fits are not very happy it seems with the background functions (the behaviour is a little bit better with --cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy=0.
I decided to go back to the data and try to run a profile likelihood scan over all of the background shape parameters, one by one. You can find them here : https://nckw.web.cern.ch/tmp/2018_sr1sr2sr3_300424_cmin1/
In each plot, a single parameter (the one on the x-axis) is scanned and the others (in the legend) are allowed to float. The ranges chosen are just ones set in the workspace which you provided. To me it looks like some of the ranges could be reduced from what we have there as the deltaNLL is very large. The jumpy behaviour worries me as in the toys this can lead to fits being extremely unstable (which is what I am seeing). How were the ranges for the parameters chosen?
Best,
Nick
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Sasha,

I was trying out a few more tests with toys, and I am noticing that the fits are not very happy it seems with the background functions (the behaviour is a little bit better with --cminDefaultMinimizerStrategy=0.

I decided to go back to the data and try to run a profile likelihood scan over all of the background shape parameters, one by one. You can find them here : https://nckw.web.cern.ch/tmp/2018_sr1sr2sr3_300424_cmin1/

In each plot, a single parameter (the one on the x-axis) is scanned and the others (in the legend) are allowed to float. The ranges chosen are just ones set in the workspace which you provided. To me it looks like some of the ranges could be reduced from what we have there as the deltaNLL is very large. The jumpy behaviour worries me as in the toys this can lead to fits being extremely unstable (which is what I am seeing). How were the ranges for the parameters chosen?

Best,
Nick

thanks for checking !
I am wondering why on your plots for parameters of the polynomial function for SR1: a1_18sr1, a2_18sr2,a3_18sr1 scan starts from 0
but not from -1.0 according to what I have in my macro for
preparing Workspace
/afs/cern.ch/work/a/anikiten/public/CMSSW_11_3_4/src/HiggsAnalysis/CombinedLimit/test/13TeV_2018UL_RooFitTest_SR1.C
other parameters are indeed in the range I set. Ranges are taken
“arbitrary large” and especially for SR2 and I do see from plots that I can restrict them. Would +/- 3 sigma work or I should take a range where
2deltaNLL is has a smoothed shape ?
Cheers, Sasha.

posts.name: Nicholas Wardle
posts.text:
Hi Sasha,
Hmm, I should check – it might be that the points below 0 for that scan failed and so don’t appear in the plot.
It might indeed be a good idea to try restricting the ranges to within +/- 3 sigma and see if things look more stable. All of this of course is part of the whole discussion on the background modelling (generally polynomials are tricky as they can go -ve, which is why we used Bernstein polynomials in the early H->gg analyses).
I’ll keep digging but I think we should think more about this as calculating a proper significance with the toys relies on having fairly robust fits for the toy datasets.
All the best,
Nick
posts.cooked_html:
Hi Sasha,

Hmm, I should check – it might be that the points below 0 for that scan failed and so don’t appear in the plot.

It might indeed be a good idea to try restricting the ranges to within +/- 3 sigma and see if things look more stable. All of this of course is part of the whole discussion on the background modelling (generally polynomials are tricky as they can go -ve, which is why we used Bernstein polynomials in the early H->gg analyses).

I’ll keep digging but I think we should think more about this as calculating a proper significance with the toys relies on having fairly robust fits for the toy datasets.

All the best,

Nick

Thanks - the error you get is indeed due to still getting -ve values issues but from the one you posted it looks like Minuit can find a way to avoid it . The number also looks more like with the --cminDefaultMinimizerStratefy 0 option which is very encouraging!
Let me know what you find from your scans, I will take your new workspaces and investigate more. Hopefully we are converging on the p-value with toys calculation now!
Best
Nick

Returning maximum FCN so far (0.335824) to force MIGRAD to back out of this region. Error log follows
Parameter values: a1_18sr1=0.0345791, a1_18sr2=6.74989, a1_18sr3=-0.576619, a2_18sr1=-0.00155, a2_18sr2=0.667131, a2_18sr3=-0.264839, a3_18sr1=1.25665e-05, a3_18sr2=-0.0333135, a3_18sr3=0.251122, a4_18sr2=0.000344881, bkgrate_sr1_18=1.0215, bkgrate_sr2_18=1.05602, bkgrate_sr3_18=1.19159, r_sig1=32.046, r_sig2=15.626, r_sig3=0.426065
RooPolynomial::shapeBkg_bkg_SR2_2018[ x=dimuonmass12 coefList=(a1_18sr2,a2_18sr2,a3_18sr2,a4_18sr2) ]
p.d.f value is less than zero (-4.343847), forcing value to zero @ x=dimuonmass12=50.5746, coefList=(a1_18sr2 = 6.74989 +/- 4.57362,a2_18sr2 = 0.667131 +/- 0.0532241,a3_18sr2 = -0.0333135 +/- 0.00234042,a4_18sr2 = 0.000344881 +/- 3.44777e-05)

With 25K events I got

– Hybrid New –
Pmu = 0.00168 +/- 0.000259012

which is close to formulae p-value: pvalue = 0.00149352

Olga will be running now 1M toys.

My new Workspace.root is at the same place if you would like to do checks:

/afs/cern.ch/work/a/anikiten/public/CMSSW_11_3_4/src/HiggsAnalysis/CombinedLimit/test/2018UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root

Cheers, Sasha.

posts.name: Alexandre Nikitenko
posts.text:
Hi Nick,
with 1M toys Olga got a p-values close with 10 % to the one with analytical
formulae. See details in
Combined 2018, SR1+SR2+SR3 significance with toys - SUS / SUS-23-011 - CMS Pub Talk (cern.ch)
We do now 2017 combination with toys after I selected parameter intervals with
scans.
/afs/cern.ch/work/a/anikiten/public/CMSSW_11_3_4/src/HiggsAnalysis/CombinedLimit/test/2017UL_dimuon_SR1SR2SR3.root
Cheers, Sasha.
